quiz_questions.question_text: An insurance premium is:

options.text: The copay at a doctor visit
The regular payment you make to keep your insurance policy active
The amount you pay before insurance kicks in
The amount of coverage you receive

quiz_questions.explanation: A premium is the periodic payment (monthly, quarterly, or annually) made to the insurance company to maintain coverage under the policy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deductible is the amount you pay:

options.text: Only for prescription drugs
Each month for coverage
Out of pocket before insurance starts covering costs
To your employer for health insurance

quiz_questions.explanation: A deductible is the initial out-of-pocket amount you must pay before insurance coverage begins. Higher deductibles typically come with lower premium costs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Higher deductible insurance plans generally have:

options.text: Lower premiums, because you accept more upfront risk
Higher premiums
No coverage until the deductible resets
The same premiums as low-deductible plans

quiz_questions.explanation: There is an inverse relationship between deductibles and premiums. By choosing a higher deductible, you take on more financial risk per claim, which lowers your regular premium payments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Health insurance coinsurance of 80/20 means:

options.text: Insurance pays 80% of covered costs and you pay 20%, after the deductible is met
You pay 80% and insurance pays 20%
Insurance covers 100% after you pay 20% of the deductible
You pay 80% of your premium

quiz_questions.explanation: With 80/20 coinsurance, after you meet your deductible, the insurer pays 80% of covered medical costs and you pay the remaining 20% until you reach the out-of-pocket maximum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Term life insurance provides:

options.text: Investment returns linked to the stock market
Lifetime coverage that never expires
A cash value savings component plus death benefit
Pure death benefit coverage for a set period (10, 20, or 30 years) at the lowest cost

quiz_questions.explanation: Term life insurance offers a death benefit for a specific time period with no cash value. It is the most affordable type of life insurance, making it ideal for income replacement during working years.

quiz_questions.question_text: Auto liability insurance covers:

options.text: Weather-related damage to your car
Damage to your own vehicle after a collision
Damage and injuries you cause to other people and their property in an accident
Theft of your vehicle

quiz_questions.explanation: Auto liability insurance is legally required in most states and covers bodily injury and property damage you cause to others in an accident. It does not cover your own vehicle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Renter's insurance costs approximately:

options.text: $1,000 per year minimum
$100 per month
$200-$500 per month
$15-$30 per month

quiz_questions.explanation: Renter's insurance is very affordable at approximately $15-$30 per month. It covers personal belongings, liability, and additional living expenses if your rental becomes uninhabitable.

quiz_questions.question_text: The recommended amount of life insurance coverage is typically:

options.text: $100,000 for everyone
10 to 12 times your annual income
$1 million regardless of income
Equal to your mortgage balance

quiz_questions.explanation: Financial advisors generally recommend 10-12 times annual income to replace the earner's income stream and cover obligations like a mortgage, children's education, and living expenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Long-term disability insurance typically replaces what percentage of income?
quiz_questions.explanation: Long-term disability policies typically replace 60-70% of pre-disability income. The benefit is less than 100% to provide an incentive to return to work when possible.

quiz_questions.question_text: Insurance works on the principle of:

options.text: Each person saving enough to cover their own risks
Guaranteed profits for everyone
Risk pooling — many people pay premiums so the few who experience losses are covered
Government subsidy of all losses

quiz_questions.explanation: Insurance pools risk across a large group. Premiums from many policyholders fund the claims of the few who experience covered losses, making catastrophic costs manageable for individuals.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'out-of-pocket maximum' in health insurance?

options.text: The maximum premium you pay per year
The total of all copays for the year
The maximum amount of coverage your plan provides
The most you will pay in a year for covered services; after reaching it, insurance pays 100%

quiz_questions.explanation: The out-of-pocket maximum is the cap on what you pay for covered services in a plan year, including deductibles, copays, and coinsurance. Once reached, the insurer covers 100%.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'comprehensive coverage' mean in auto insurance?

options.text: Full replacement of your vehicle regardless of fault
Coverage for non-collision damage such as theft, vandalism, weather, fire, and animal strikes
Coverage for all possible situations including collisions
The most expensive insurance package available

quiz_questions.explanation: Comprehensive auto coverage pays for damage to your vehicle from causes other than collision, including theft, vandalism, hail, fire, flooding, and animal strikes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'whole life insurance'?

options.text: Permanent life insurance with a death benefit and a cash value component that grows over time, costing significantly more than term
A type of health insurance that covers all medical expenses
Life insurance that covers you for a specific number of years
Insurance that covers your entire family under one policy

quiz_questions.explanation: Whole life insurance provides lifelong coverage with level premiums and includes a cash value component that grows tax-deferred. It costs 5-15 times more than a comparable term policy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'collision coverage' mean in auto insurance?

options.text: Coverage for medical bills after a collision
Coverage that pays for the other driver's vehicle in an accident
Coverage only when another driver hits your parked car
Coverage that pays to repair or replace your vehicle after an accident, regardless of who was at fault

quiz_questions.explanation: Collision coverage pays for repairs to your own vehicle after a collision with another vehicle or object, minus your deductible, regardless of fault.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'umbrella insurance policy'?

options.text: Insurance that specifically covers weather damage
A policy that bundles home, auto, and life insurance into one plan
A short-term policy that provides temporary coverage
Extra liability coverage that kicks in above the limits of your auto and homeowner's policies, typically providing $1-5 million in additional protection

quiz_questions.explanation: An umbrella policy provides additional liability coverage beyond what your existing auto and homeowner's policies offer. It protects against large lawsuits or claims that exceed standard policy limits.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'disability insurance' protect?

options.text: Your ability to earn income by providing replacement payments if an illness or injury prevents you from working
Your home from natural disasters, despite what intuition might initially suggest
Your investments from market losses when considering all relevant disability factors
Your vehicle from theft, even when conditions shift significantly

quiz_questions.explanation: Disability insurance protects your greatest asset — your earning capacity. You are statistically more likely to become disabled during your working years than to die, making this coverage critical.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'risk pooling' in insurance?

options.text: An investment strategy that diversifies across asset classes
A method where each person saves individually for potential losses
A government program that insures all citizens equally
Combining the premiums of many policyholders to fund the claims of the few who experience losses, spreading financial risk

quiz_questions.explanation: Risk pooling is the fundamental concept behind insurance. Large groups of people contribute premiums, creating a fund that pays out claims for the relatively small number who experience covered losses.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'copay' mean in health insurance?

options.text: The total amount you pay before insurance begins
The monthly premium payment
A fixed dollar amount you pay at the time of receiving a specific medical service, such as a doctor visit or prescription
The percentage of costs shared between insurer and insured after the deductible

quiz_questions.explanation: A copay is a fixed fee (e.g., $25 for a doctor visit, $15 for a generic prescription) paid at the time of service. It differs from coinsurance, which is a percentage of the cost.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'renter's insurance'?

options.text: A policy required by all states for renters
Insurance that covers the building structure of a rental property
Insurance the landlord buys to protect against tenant damage
A policy that protects a renter's personal belongings, provides liability coverage, and covers additional living expenses if the unit becomes uninhabitable

quiz_questions.explanation: Renter's insurance protects tenants' personal property, provides personal liability coverage, and often includes loss-of-use benefits. The landlord's policy covers the building structure, not tenant belongings.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'beneficiary' mean in insurance?

options.text: The person or entity designated to receive the insurance payout or death benefit
The insurance agent who sold the policy
The policyholder who pays the premiums
The insurance company providing the coverage

quiz_questions.explanation: A beneficiary is the person or entity named in a policy to receive the benefits. In life insurance, the beneficiary receives the death benefit when the insured person passes away.

quiz_questions.question_text: Plan A has a $300/month premium and $500 deductible. Plan B has a $200/month premium and $2,000 deductible. If you expect $3,000 in medical expenses this year, which plan costs less total?

options.text: Plan B — $4,400 total
They cost exactly the same
Plan A — $4,100 total
Plan B — $4,100 total

quiz_questions.explanation: Plan A: $300 x 12 + $500 deductible = $4,100. Plan B: $200 x 12 + $2,000 deductible = $4,400. With $3,000 in expected expenses, Plan A saves $300 despite the higher monthly premium.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sarah has health insurance with a $1,500 deductible, 80/20 coinsurance, and $6,000 out-of-pocket maximum. She has a surgery costing $25,000. How much does she pay out of pocket?
quiz_questions.explanation: She pays the $1,500 deductible first. Remaining $23,500: she owes 20% = $4,700. But $1,500 + $4,700 = $6,200 exceeds the $6,000 out-of-pocket max. So she pays the $6,000 cap and insurance covers the rest.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 30-year-old can buy a $500,000 20-year term life policy for $25/month or a $500,000 whole life policy for $350/month. If they buy term and invest the $325/month difference at 8% annual return for 20 years, approximately how much would the investment grow to?
quiz_questions.explanation: Using the future value of annuity formula: $325/month for 20 years at 8% annual (0.667% monthly) yields approximately $191,000. This illustrates the 'buy term and invest the difference' strategy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Mike earns $85,000 per year and wants life insurance coverage equal to 10 times his salary. He can get a 20-year term policy for $0.50 per $1,000 of coverage per month. What is his monthly premium?
quiz_questions.explanation: Coverage needed = $85,000 x 10 = $850,000. Number of $1,000 units = 850. Monthly premium = 850 x $0.50 = $425 per month.

quiz_questions.question_text: Amy has auto insurance with 100/300/100 liability limits. She causes an accident injuring three people with medical bills of $120,000, $80,000, and $50,000. How much will her insurance pay for bodily injury?

options.text: $200,000 — limited by the per-person cap on each claimant
$300,000 — the per-accident limit
$250,000 — the total of all claims
$230,000 — $100,000 for the first person, $80,000 for the second, $50,000 for the third

quiz_questions.explanation: 100/300 means $100,000 max per person and $300,000 max per accident. Person 1 gets $100,000 (capped), Person 2 gets $80,000, Person 3 gets $50,000. Total = $230,000, which is under the $300,000 per-accident limit.

quiz_questions.question_text: A family pays $600/month for health insurance with a $2,000 deductible, and they typically spend $5,000/year in medical costs. If they switch to a $400/month plan with a $5,000 deductible and no expected medical events, which plan is cheaper for the year?

options.text: Both plans cost the same
The $400/month plan at $4,800 total (premiums only, no medical events)
The $600/month plan at $9,200 total
The $400/month plan at $9,800 total

quiz_questions.explanation: With no medical events, the $400/month plan costs only $4,800 in premiums. The $600/month plan costs $7,200 in premiums alone. High-deductible plans save money when you rarely use medical services.

quiz_questions.question_text: Derek earns $70,000/year and becomes disabled. His employer-provided disability insurance replaces 60% of income with a 90-day waiting period. How much will he receive monthly once benefits start?
quiz_questions.explanation: Annual benefit = $70,000 x 0.60 = $42,000. Monthly benefit = $42,000 / 12 = $3,500. He receives no payments during the first 90 days (elimination period) and must cover expenses from savings.

quiz_questions.question_text: Lisa's apartment is burglarized and she loses $8,000 worth of electronics and jewelry. Her renter's insurance has a $500 deductible. She pays $20/month for the policy. How much net benefit does she receive, and what is her effective cost for one year of coverage?

options.text: $8,000 reimbursement; $240 annual premium cost
$7,500 reimbursement; $500 annual premium cost
$7,500 reimbursement; $240 annual premium cost
$7,260 reimbursement after premium deduction

quiz_questions.explanation: Insurance pays $8,000 minus $500 deductible = $7,500. Annual premium = $20 x 12 = $240. The $240 annual cost protected her against $7,500 in losses — an excellent return on the insurance investment.

quiz_questions.question_text: A homeowner with a $500,000 house and $300,000 auto policy causes an accident resulting in a $750,000 lawsuit. Without umbrella coverage, they face $450,000 in personal liability. A $1 million umbrella policy costs $300/year. Should they have purchased it?

options.text: No, their auto policy should have covered the full amount
Yes, but only if the lawsuit exceeds $1 million
Yes — $300/year would have covered the $450,000 gap, protecting their assets from a devastating judgment
No, umbrella policies are unnecessary for average homeowners

quiz_questions.explanation: The auto policy covers $300,000, leaving $450,000 unprotected. A $1 million umbrella at $300/year would have covered the entire gap. For homeowners with significant assets, umbrella coverage is critical protection.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple is comparing two health plans for their family of four. Plan X: $800/month premium, $1,000 deductible, 80/20 coinsurance, $8,000 out-of-pocket max. Plan Y: $500/month premium, $4,000 deductible, 70/30 coinsurance, $12,000 out-of-pocket max. They expect $15,000 in medical costs this year. Which plan costs less total?

options.text: Plan Y at $12,300 total
Both plans cost exactly $12,000
Plan X at $12,400 total
Plan Y at $9,700 total

quiz_questions.explanation: Plan X: $9,600 premiums + $1,000 deductible + 20% of $14,000 = $2,800, but capped at $8,000 OOP max. Total = $9,600 + min(cost-sharing, cap). Plan Y: $6,000 premiums + $4,000 deductible + 30% of $11,000 = $3,300. Total = $6,000 + $7,300 = $13,300. Plan X's out-of-pocket cap makes it cheaper for high medical usage.
